title: `[docs]` `tauri-bundler` README links Tauri CLI to nonexistent `../cli`
author: m-novotny
body:
### Documentation location

`crates/tauri-bundler/README.md`:

https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/11012a13f55ab55ec5ed12ca3ac95338ae1731c3/crates/tauri-bundler/README.md#L7

### Problem

The `Tauri CLI` link targets `../cli`, which does not exist, so the link returns 404. The CLI crate in this workspace is `crates/tauri-cli`.

### Suggested fix

Change the link target from `../cli` to `../tauri-cli`.

I did not find an existing issue or PR for this README path.

title: RUSTSEC-2026-0206: `rustybuzz` is unmaintained
author: github-actions[bot]
body:
> `rustybuzz` is unmaintained

| Details             |                                                |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Status              | unmaintained                |
| Package             | `rustybuzz`                      |
| Version             | `0.20.1`                   |
| URL                 | [https://github.com/harfbuzz/rustybuzz/issues/166](https://github.com/harfbuzz/rustybuzz/issues/166) |
| Date                | 2026-07-11                         |

The current maintainer of `rustybuzz` has stated that the crate is unmaintained and will not receive further fixes or updates (see the referenced issue).

## Alternative(s)

- [`harfrust`](https://github.com/harfbuzz/harfrust), an actively maintained and updated alternative which is part of the Harfbuzz project.

See [advisory page](https://rustsec.org/advisories/RUSTSEC-2026-0206.html) for additional details.

title: [bug] rpm bundling hangs indefinitely on current versions (2.11.4 / tauri-bundler 2.9.4) — reproduces in GitHub Actions CI
author: Just-Insane
body:
Likely the same underlying issue as #11478 (closed not-planned, no root cause identified), reproducible essentially 100% of the time for us on current versions — opening fresh since that one's closed and this includes new diagnostic evidence that wasn't captured there.

**Versions**: `@tauri-apps/cli` 2.11.4, `tauri-bundler` 2.9.4, reproduced in GitHub Actions CI (`ubuntu-latest`/`ubuntu-22.04`), command: `pnpm tauri build --debug --bundles deb,rpm`.

**Symptom**: identical to #11478 — deb bundles fine, rpm bundling starts ("Bundling foo-0.1.0-1.x86_64.rpm" prints), then the process goes completely silent forever. No error, no CPU usage, no timeout from the tool itself — needs to be killed externally.

### What we ruled out

Each independently tested and falsified via a background-build + live `/proc` snapshot on the actual hung GitHub Actions runner:

- FUSE mounting for AppImage — doesn't apply; the hang reproduces identically with `--bundles deb,rpm` (AppImage excluded entirely).
- An untimed network download inside `tauri-bundler`'s `http_utils::download` (`ureq`) — pre-fetching the AppImage tooling into `~/.cache/tauri` so that code path never runs didn't change anything.
- A libuv `io_uring` kernel deadlock (the stuck process had `anon_inode:[io_uring]` fds open) — `UV_USE_IO_URING=0` didn't fix it, and the actual `wchan` for every relevant thread was plain `ep_poll`, not an `io_uring`-specific wait.
- A recent Node.js regression — pinning `actions/setup-node` to `22.22.3` (the version before the `22.23.0`/`22.23.1` June 2026 releases) didn't fix it either.
- Runner image drift — reproduces identically on both `ubuntu-24.04` (kernel `6.17.0-1018-azure`) and `ubuntu-22.04` (kernel `6.8.0-1059-azure`).
- A byte-for-byte reproduction of our own CI job as it existed before we made any recent caching/tooling changes (when it was apparently passing reliably) — still hangs today under otherwise-identical conditions, ruling out anything in our own workflow config.

### What's consistent across every hang

The process tree never includes any subprocess beyond the Tauri CLI's own `node` process — nothing external ever gets spawned for the rpm step, consistent with RPM generation happening in-process via the `rpm` crate (`rpm::PackageBuilder`), not a shelled-out `rpmbuild`. Every thread of that node process is idle (zero CPU) — main thread parked in `epoll_pwait`, worker threads in `futex_wait` — when snapshotted mid-hang.

### Narrowed further with `-vv`

We hadn't captured bundler-internal verbose output during an actual hang until this run. With `-v -v`, the very last log line before it goes silent is:

```
Debug [handlebars::render] Rendering value: Path(Relative(([Named("mime_type")], "mime_type")))
```

— the final step of rendering the `.desktop` file template for the **rpm** target (`freedesktop::generate_desktop_file`, called from `rpm.rs`). Notably, the identical handlebars render sequence completes successfully for the **deb** target earlier in the same run (deb bundles fine every time). So the hang isn't in `handlebars` or desktop-file generation itself — it's somewhere in what runs immediately after, in `rpm.rs`, none of which appears to log anything at `-vv`:

- `list_icon_files` (opens each configured PNG icon and decodes its header via `image::codecs::png::PngDecoder` to get dimensions)
- adding files to the `rpm::PackageBuilder` (`.with_file(...)`)
- the final `builder.build()` call (cpio archive assembly + compression — default Gzip level 6, no custom `rpm` config on our end, no `TAURI_SIGNING_RPM_KEY` set so PGP signing is not in play)

Given the process is idle (zero CPU) rather than busy-spinning when snapshotted, a raw computational hang inside PNG decoding or gzip compression seems less likely than something in that range blocking on an event that never arrives — but we don't have log coverage to point at one specific line yet.

This appears to be environment/kernel-adjacent rather than anything malformed in our config, and is a real CI blocker — currently only mitigated on our end with a retry-on-timeout wrapper, not a real fix. Happy to capture more (we now have decent tooling for getting a live `/proc`/strace snapshot of the exact hung process) — let us know what would help narrow this down further, e.g. instrumenting `rpm.rs`'s `list_icon_files`/`with_file`/`build()` calls with explicit log lines for a future release.

title: [bug] `wix.fipsCompliant` only passes `-fips` to candle.exe, so MSI bundling still fails at light.exe (LGHT0001) on FIPS-enabled machines
author: zts3y
body:
### Describe the bug

On a Windows machine with the FIPS security policy enabled (common on
corporate images: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\FipsAlgorithmPolicy\Enabled = 1`),
MSI bundling cannot succeed even with the dedicated config option enabled:

1. Without `bundle.windows.wix.fipsCompliant`, candle fails as expected:
   `error CNDL0308: FIPS appears to be enabled on the machine.`
2. Setting `"bundle": { "windows": { "wix": { "fipsCompliant": true } } }`
   fixes candle — but the build then dies one stage later:
   `light.exe : error LGHT0001 : Catastrophic failure`
   (`failed to bundle project: error running light.exe`).

Cause: the bundler adds `-fips` to the **candle** argument list only. In
`crates/tauri-bundler/src/bundle/windows/msi/mod.rs`, `run_candle()` has:

```rust
if settings
  .windows()
  .wix
  .as_ref()
  .map(|w| w.fips_compliant)
  .unwrap_or_default()
{
  args.push("-fips".into());
}
```

but `run_light()` never receives the flag. light.exe hashes files too
(smart cabbing uses MD5 by default) and accepts the same `-fips` switch,
so on a FIPS machine it hits the non-FIPS-validated algorithm and dies
with the generic LGHT0001. Since there is also no passthrough for custom
light.exe arguments, there is currently **no way to build an MSI with
Tauri on a FIPS-enabled machine** (NSIS works fine, which is the
workaround we're using).

This is still the case on current `dev` (checked
`crates/tauri-bundler/src/bundle/windows/msi/mod.rs` on `dev` as of
2026-07-10).

### Reproduction

### Reproduction

On any Windows machine with the FIPS policy enabled
(`HKLM\...\FipsAlgorithmPolicy\Enabled = 1`):

1. `npm create tauri-app@latest` (defaults are fine)
2. Add to `src-tauri/tauri.conf.json`:
   `"bundle": { "windows": { "wix": { "fipsCompliant": true } }, "targets": ["msi"] }`
3. `npm run tauri build`

candle passes, then: `light.exe : error LGHT0001 : Catastrophic failure`.

### Expected behavior

`fipsCompliant: true` (or the `TAURI_BUNDLER_WIX_FIPS_COMPLIANT` env var)
should make the whole WiX pipeline FIPS-safe — i.e. pass `-fips` to
light.exe with the same conditional that `run_candle()` already uses.
Looks like a one-line fix in `run_light()`.

### Full `tauri info` output

```text
[✔] Environment
    - OS: Windows 10.0.26100 x86_64 (X64)
    ✔ WebView2: 150.0.4078.48
    ✔ MSVC: Visual Studio Build Tools 2026
    ✔ rustc: 1.97.0 (2d8144b78 2026-07-07)
    ✔ cargo: 1.97.0 (c980f4866 2026-06-30)
    ✔ rustup: 1.29.0 (28d1352db 2026-03-05)
    ✔ Rust toolchain: stable-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc (default)
    - node: 24.18.0
    - npm: 11.16.0

[-] Packages
    - tauri [RUST]: 2.11.5
    - tauri-build [RUST]: 2.6.3
    - wry [RUST]: 0.55.1
    - tao [RUST]: 0.35.3
    - @tauri-apps/api [NPM]: 2.11.1
    - @tauri-apps/cli [NPM]: 2.11.4

[-] Plugins
    - tauri-plugin-fs [RUST]: 2.5.1
    - @tauri-apps/plugin-fs [NPM]: 2.5.1
    - tauri-plugin-log [RUST]: 2.8.0
    - @tauri-apps/plugin-log [NPM]: not installed!
    - tauri-plugin-dialog [RUST]: 2.7.1
    - @tauri-apps/plugin-dialog [NPM]: 2.7.1

[-] App
    - build-type: bundle
    - CSP: default-src 'self'; frame-src 'self' https:; connect-src 'self' ipc: http://ipc.localhost http://127.0.0.1:*; media-src 'self' asset: http://asset.localhost blob:; img-src 'self' asset: http://asset.localhost data: blob: https://img.youtube.com https://i.ytimg.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'self'; font-src 'self' data:; worker-src 'self' blob:
    - frontendDist: ../dist
    - devUrl: http://127.0.0.1:5173/
    - framework: React
    - bundler: Vite
```

### Stack trace

```text

```

### Additional context

### Additional context

- Reproduced with `@tauri-apps/cli` 2.11.4 and WiX 3.14 as auto-downloaded
  by Tauri to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\tauri\WixTools314`.
- WiX v3's candle/light both document the `-fips` switch; prior art on the
  switch pairing: https://wix-users.narkive.com/OcXVS3sb/bundle-creation-error-using-fips
- Related but distinct: #14681 / #12148 (the latter is why the LGHT/CNDL
  error text is easy to miss — the wrapped error is just
  "failed to bundle project").

title: [bug] Verbose logging output during code signing (using default `tauri build`)
author: WillemJann
body:
### Describe the bug

When we execute `tauri build`, we receive a very large number of log messages related to code signing during the bundling process. As a result, the output becomes almost unreadable (> 3000 lines). We noticed that detailed information about code signing appears in the normal output. This issue is easily remedied by changing the level of log messages regarding code signing so that they only appear with a `tauri build -v`. The many log messages contain useful information, but are only relevant with a log-level verbose.

### Reproduction

Create a .NET API project and publish it as a self-contained application. This generates >300 files (.DLL and .EXE), most of which are already signed, while a few are not. Place these files in a `bin` folder in the `src-tauri` directory and configure the following in the `tauri.conf.json`:

```
"bundle": {
    "resources": {
        "bin/*": ""
    }
}
```

Next, enable code signing (in our example we use Azure Artifact Signing, but this applies to any code signing method). Then run `tauri build` and inspect the terminal output. You will see a lot of debug/informational messages.

### Expected behavior

For already signed files, the following output should not be visible and only appear in verbose mode:

```
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clretwrc.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clretwrc.dll

========================================

File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clrgcexp.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clrgcexp.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clrjit.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\clrjit.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\coreclr.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\coreclr.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\createdump.exe
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\createdump.exe
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\hostfxr.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\hostfxr.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\hostpolicy.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\hostpolicy.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordaccore.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordaccore.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordaccore_amd64_amd64_10.0.926.27113.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordaccore_amd64_amd64_10.0.926.27113.dll
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordbi.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
0      sha256     RFC3161      

Successfully verified: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\mscordbi.dll

========================================
```

For unsigned files, the following output:

```
File: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll
Index  Algorithm  Timestamp    
========================================
SignTool Error: No signature found.

Number of errors: 1
     Signing C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll
     Signing C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll with a custom signing command
     Signing Output of signing command:
[
  {
    "cloudName": "AzureCloud",
    "homeTenantId": "<...>",
    "id": "<...>",
    "isDefault": true,
    "managedByTenants": [],
    "name": "Azure subscription 1",
    "state": "Enabled",
    "tenantId": "<...>",
    "user": {
      "name": "<...>",
      "type": "servicePrincipal"
    }
  }
]

Artifact Signing

Version: 1.0.128

"Metadata": {
  "Endpoint": "https://neu.codesigning.azure.net/",
  "CodeSigningAccountName": "<...>",
  "CertificateProfileName": "<...>",
  "ExcludeCredentials": []
}

Submitting digest for signing...

OperationId d2718ad2-7586-4acc-9766-0fcf0c4c5fa7: InProgress

Signing completed with status 'Succeeded' in 3.5060853s

Successfully signed: C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll

Number of files successfully Signed: 1
Number of warnings: 0
Number of errors: 0
```

Should be summarized to:

```
     Signing C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll
     Signing C:\Users\Willem-Jan\tauri-app\src-tauri\bin\Contoso.API.dll with a custom signing command
```

In our real situation, this is the current output compared to the expected output:

[Current output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29849337/Current.output.txt) (3180 lines)
[Expected output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29849339/Expected.output.txt) (87 lines)

Having a more compressed output, this will help us a lot in understanding the result of the `tauri build` action.

### Full `tauri info` output

```text
[✔] Environment
    - OS: Windows 10.0.26200 x86_64 (X64)
    ✔ WebView2: 150.0.4078.48
    ✔ MSVC: Visual Studio Enterprise 2026
```

title: [bug] Cursor contrast calculation uses empty frame buffer
author: SimenZhor
body:
### Describe the bug

Let me start off by saying I'm fairly certain this is not a bug in Tauri or wry, but since that's how I discovered it I'm reporting it anyway as I'm not sure if there's something that can be done to mitigate the problem (and also to publish my workaround for others who may have the same issue).

In quite specific conditions I'm having trouble with the cursor "disappearing" over a white background. After a lot of debugging I believe this happens because the automatic contrast calculation XORs the cursor with an all black frame buffer - which makes the cursor white.

The following conditions need to be met ([example project](https://github.com/SimenZhor/cursor-bug-demo)):
- (AMD) hardware accelerated cursor contrast calculation (for me this means that I can't have external monitors connected to my laptop, as that triggers a software implementation where this bug is not present)
- A `crosshair` or `grab`/`grabbing` cursor needs to be used (maybe some others too, but `pointer` does not have this issue)
- Frequent `requestAnimationFrame` calls somewhere in the window.
- The background below the cursor needs to be white or something else with low contrast against an unintentionally white cursor.
- I've only seen the bug on Windows.

For me, the criterion above occurred naturally (and reliably) in a fairly light-colored app where I had a React Three Fiber canvas as a small preview in my layout (I've since noticed that r3f canvas defaults to continuously redraw at 60 Hz, and that this can be prevented with [on demand rendering](https://r3f.docs.pmnd.rs/advanced/scaling-performance#on-demand-rendering), but that was not enough to fix the issue in my case).

I've not been able to capture screenshots or recordings of the issue, as the tools I've tried to use for capturing it seem to force a software-cursor instead of the hardware-cursor where the problem appears (in fact it's even worse: I can see the bug occurring as I record, but the resulting recording shows the correct, high-contrast, cursor 🙄).

**Workarounds**

I've found two workarounds for the problem, which makes me quite certain that this is a hardware related issue (note: these do not need to be active at the same time):
- Setting the following registry key to disable multi-plane overlay (MPO) `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Dwm\OverlayTestMode=5`. This is obviously just diagnostic evidence, not a real fix.
- Adding the following option to `tauri.conf.json`: 
```json
"app": {
  "windows": [
    {
      "additionalBrowserArgs": "--disable-direct-composition"
    }
  ],
},
```

The latter is a fix that can be distributed, so I guess I'm fine with it for my personal needs, but since it feels like I'm just hiding an issue that is still present I decided to file this bug report.

### Reproduction

I've set up a minimal example where the bug can be triggered by rapidly moving the cursor above the white square in the canvas. 

Note that the bug is hardware dependent, so it will probably not trigger consistently for everyone running the demo.
https://github.com/SimenZhor/cursor-bug-demo

### Expected behavior

The cursor is black when hovering over a white background.

### Full `tauri info` output

```text
$ tauri info

[✔] Environment
    - OS: Windows 10.0.26200 x86_64 (X64)
    ✔ WebView2: 150.0.4078.48
    ✔ MSVC: 
        - Visual Studio Build Tools 2019
        - Visual Studio Build Tools 2022
    ✔ rustc: 1.91.0 (f8297e351 2025-10-28)
    ✔ cargo: 1.91.0 (ea2d97820 2025-10-10)
    ✔ rustup: 1.28.2 (e4f3ad6f8 2025-04-28)
    ✔ Rust toolchain: stable-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc (default)
    - node: 22.13.1
    - pnpm: 10.3.0
    - yarn: 1.22.22
    - npm: 10.9.2
    - bun: 1.3.7

[-] Packages
    - tauri 🦀: 2.11.5
    - tauri-build 🦀: 2.6.3
    - wry 🦀: 0.55.1
    - tao 🦀: 0.35.3
    - @tauri-apps/api  ⱼₛ: 2.11.1
    - @tauri-apps/cli  ⱼₛ: 2.11.4

[-] Plugins
    - tauri-plugin-opener 🦀: 2.5.4
    - @tauri-apps/plugin-opener  ⱼₛ: 2.5.4

[-] App
    - build-type: bundle
    - CSP: unset
    - frontendDist: ../dist
    - devUrl: http://localhost:1420/
    - bundler: Vite
```

title: RUSTSEC-2026-0205: `Array::insert` violates exception safety if compare function panics, leading to potential Double-Free
author: github-actions[bot]
body:
> `Array::insert` violates exception safety if compare function panics, leading to potential Double-Free

| Details             |                                                |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Status              | unsound                |
| Package             | `scc`                      |
| Version             | `2.4.0`                   |
| URL                 | [https://codeberg.org/wvwwvwwv/scalable-concurrent-containers/issues/232](https://codeberg.org/wvwwvwwv/scalable-concurrent-containers/issues/232) |
| Date                | 2026-07-06                         |

In affected versions of this crate, `Array::insert` is not exception safe. In the `UPSERT` path, `key` and `value`'s snapshot is written to `self.data_block`, and after the insertion is completed, `key` and `value` are `mem::forget` in order to prevent double free. However, during the insertion, `K::compare` is invoked, which is a user-provided method. If user deliberately call panic in this function, during unwinding, the destructors of `key`, `value`, and `self` will all be called (since the `mem::forget` has not been called yet), leading to Double Free. Similar issues happens in the `!UPSERT` path.

The soundness issue was fixed in version `3.8.4` by using `ManuallyDrop` instead of `mem::forget`, and separating fallible code from infallible code during a node split - insert = insert_try -> insert_unchecked.

See [advisory page](https://rustsec.org/advisories/RUSTSEC-2026-0205.html) for additional details.

title: [bug] Android: blank webview when relaunching the app after task removal while a foreground service keeps the process alive
author: Turbo87
body:
### Describe the bug When a foreground service keeps the app process alive and the user swipes the app away from recents and then relaunches it from the launcher, the relaunched activity stays a plain white screen. No webview is created, JS never executes (zero console output). A second relaunch does not recover, only killing the process does. The Rust side keeps running fine the whole time. Reproduced with current versions (tauri 2.11.5, tao 0.35.3, wry 0.55.1) on Android 14 and 15 emulator images. Minimal standalone reproduction below. Possibly related: #11609 reported this scenario (foreground service keeps the process alive, app unusable on next launch). The activity memory leak from that issue was fixed and the issue was closed after the original reproduction disappeared, but the relaunch case itself is still broken. The `__TAURI_INVOKE_KEY__` errors quoted there came from wry's process-wide statics in pre-leak-fix versions. On current versions no webview is created at all, so there is nothing left to log. The surface symptom changed along the way, but the scenario is the same: a process that outlives its activity does not get a working webview back. #### Root cause Three layers are involved when a foreground service keeps the process alive and the user swipes the app away from recents, then relaunches it: 1. Without countermeasures the process does not even survive: destroying the last activity closes the webview window and Tauri's default exit-on-last-window-close makes tao's Android `EventLoop::run` call `std::process::exit(0)`, killing the FGS with it. An app can prevent that with `RunEvent::ExitRequested` + `api.prevent_exit()` (used in the reproduction below). 2. tao maps one Android Activity to one tao `Window` (`WindowId == ActivityId`), and `Window::new` binds to the "next available activity" via `ndk_glue::next_available_activity()`. wry can even re-create a webview when an activity with a known id comes back (`android_setup` replays stored `WEBVIEW_ATTRIBUTES`), but the id in the generated `WryActivity.onCreate` is `savedInstanceState ?: intent extras ?: hashCode()`. A launcher relaunch after task removal has neither saved state nor the extra, so the new activity gets a fresh `hashCode()`, nothing matches, and no webview is ever created for it. The relaunched activity stays blank and unclaimed. 3. The app cannot react on its own either: in `tauri-runtime-wry` the mobile `e @ Event::Resumed | e @ Event::Suspended` arm dispatches only per-window events. With zero windows the loop body never runs, so the resume is silently dropped. App-level `RunEvent::Resumed` only fires from `NewEvents(StartCause::Poll)`, which never happens here. ### Reproduction ```sh npm create tauri-app@latest tauri-11609-repro -- --template vanilla-ts \ --manager npm --identifier com.example.repro --yes cd tauri-11609-repro && npm install ``` Keep the process alive across activity destruction, like any app with a foreground service needs to. In `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`, replace the `.run(...)` call of the template with: ```rust .build(tauri::generate_context!()) .expect("error while building tauri application") .run(|_app, event| { if let tauri::RunEvent::ExitRequested { api, .. } = &event { api.prevent_exit(); } }); ``` Then `npm run tauri android init` and add a minimal foreground service to the generated project (this stands in for any real FGS, e.g. location tracking):
src-tauri/gen/android/app/src/main/java/com/example/repro/KeepAliveService.kt (new file) ```kotlin package com.example.repro import android.app.Notification import android.app.NotificationChannel import android.app.NotificationManager import android.app.Service import android.content.Intent import android.os.IBinder class KeepAliveService : Service() { override fun onStartCommand(intent: Intent?, flags: Int, startId: Int): Int { val nm = getSystemService(NotificationManager::class.java) nm.createNotificationChannel( NotificationChannel("keepalive", "Keep alive", NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_LOW) ) val notification = Notification.Builder(this, "keepalive") .setContentTitle("running") .setSmallIcon(android.R.drawable.ic_menu_info_details) .build() startForeground(1, notification) return START_STICKY } override fun onBind(intent: Intent?): IBinder? = null } ```
MainActivity.kt (start the service) and AndroidManifest.xml (permissions + service) ```kotlin package com.example.repro import android.content.Intent import android.os.Bundle class MainActivity : TauriActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) startForegroundService(Intent(this, KeepAliveService::class.java)) } } ``` ```xml ```
Run it and reproduce: ```sh npm run tauri android build -- --debug --apk adb install -r src-tauri/gen/android/app/build/outputs/apk/universal/debug/app-universal-debug.apk adb shell am start -n com.example.repro/.MainActivity # renders the template UI # swipe the app away from recents (activity destroyed, FGS keeps the process): adb shell input keyevent KEYCODE_APP_SWITCH && sleep 2 && adb shell input swipe 540 1080 540 150 250 adb shell pidof com.example.repro # still alive adb shell am start -n com.example.repro/.MainActivity # relaunch ``` (The swipe coordinates fit the 1080x2400 pixel_7 emulator image, on other devices dismiss the recents card manually.) Result: blank white screen, no webview content, no JS execution. A second relaunch does not recover, only killing the process does. The Rust side keeps running fine the whole time. ### Expected behavior Relaunching the app while the FGS-pinned process is still alive shows a working webview again, like a fresh cold start does. ### Full `tauri info` output ```text [✔] Environment - OS: Mac OS 15.7.4 arm64 (X64) ✔ Xcode Command Line Tools: installed ✔ Xcode: 26.3 ✔ rustc: 1.96.0 (ac68faa20 2026-05-25) ✔ cargo: 1.96.0 (30a34c682 2026-05-25) ✔ rustup: 1.29.0 (28d1352db 2026-03-05) ✔ Rust toolchain: stable-aarch64-apple-darwin (default) - node: 24.11.1 - pnpm: 11.2.2 - yarn: 1.22.22 - npm: 11.6.2 - bun: 1.2.17 [-] Packages - tauri 🦀: 2.11.5 - tauri-build 🦀: 2.6.3 - wry 🦀: 0.55.1 - tao 🦀: 0.35.3 - tauri-cli 🦀: 2.11.4 - @tauri-apps/api ⱼₛ: 2.11.1 - @tauri-apps/cli ⱼₛ: 2.11.4 [-] Plugins - tauri-plugin-opener 🦀: 2.5.4 - @tauri-apps/plugin-opener ⱼₛ: 2.5.4 [-] App - build-type: bundle - CSP: unset - frontendDist: ../dist - devUrl: http://localhost:1420/ - bundler: Vite ``` ### Stack trace ```text Nothing is logged when the blank activity comes up. That is part of the problem, the resume is silently dropped (see root cause above). ``` ### Additional context #### Fix, validated end-to-end Two parts. First, surface the resume to the app in `tauri-runtime-wry` (in the mobile `Resumed`/`Suspended` arm): ```rust if matches!(e, Event::Resumed) { callback(RunEvent::Resumed); } ``` (To try this against the published release, copy `tauri-runtime-wry` 2.11.4 out of the cargo registry, add the three lines at the top of the arm, and point a `[patch.crates-io]` entry at the copy. The `dev` branch has the identical code in this arm.) Second, re-create the window when resumed with no webviews. The repro's `run` closure from above becomes: ```rust .run(|app, event| match event { tauri::RunEvent::ExitRequested { api, .. } => { api.prevent_exit(); } tauri::RunEvent::Resumed => { use tauri::Manager; if app.webview_windows().is_empty() { tauri::WebviewWindowBuilder::new(app, "main", tauri::WebviewUrl::default()) .build() .expect("failed to recreate webview window"); } } _ => {} }); ``` The builder claims the relaunched activity through the existing `next_available_activity()` machinery. With these two changes the exact repro above relaunches into a fully working UI: the page renders, `invoke` round-trips work (verified with the template's `greet` command), and in a larger app I verified a foreground-service `Channel` stream staying gap-free across three swipe-away/relaunch cycles in a single process. #### Proposal Part two shouldn't be every app's problem. Tauri core could do it: on Android, when `Resumed` arrives and no webviews exist, re-run the same config-window loop that `setup()` already uses (`WebviewWindowBuilder::from_config` for windows with `create: true`). Zero webviews while resumed is never a healthy state on Android, so I'd argue for default-on rather than a config flag, but happy to gate it if you prefer. One thing worth a docs note either way: the recreated webview starts with fresh frontend state, apps keeping background state in Rust need to re-sync on load. I'm happy to submit a PR for both parts.

title: AppImages from default bundler settings fail on Mesa 25+ distros (over-bundled libwayland/glib/gstreamer, stale GST_PLUGIN_SYSTEM_PATH in AppRun)
author: wpfleger96
body:
### Describe the bug

I hit this with a tauri 2.11 app ([block/buzz](https://github.com/block/buzz)) shipped as an AppImage built with the default bundler settings (no `bundle.linux` config at all, built on ubuntu-24.04 GitHub runners). On newer distros — Ubuntu 26.04 with Mesa 25 and GLib 2.88 in my case — the AppImage starts but the window never appears: WebKitWebProcess aborts and the app keeps running headless with no error surfaced to the user.

I spent a while bisecting this and it comes down to three separate mechanisms, all in the default AppImage bundling path (`crates/tauri-bundler/src/bundle/linux/appimage/linuxdeploy.rs` plus the vendored linuxdeploy-plugin-gtk):

**1. `libwayland-client` gets bundled, which breaks newer Mesa.** linuxdeploy's dependency follower sweeps `libwayland-client.so.0` (and the glib family that pins it) into `usr/lib` — its excludelist doesn't contain it, though the pkg2appimage community excludelist does, with a comment that bundling it breaks newer Mesa. With the host's Mesa 25 loaded against the bundled libwayland-client 1.22, `eglGetDisplay(EGL_DEFAULT_DISPLAY)` fails with `EGL_BAD_PARAMETER` under a Wayland session, WebKitWebProcess prints "Could not create default EGL display: EGL_BAD_PARAMETER. Aborting..." and dies. I confirmed by LD_PRELOAD-bisecting the bundled libs one at a time: every lib that reproduced the EGL failure links `libwayland-client`, and removing the wayland/glib infra libs fixes it.

**2. AppRun exports `GST_PLUGIN_SYSTEM_PATH_1_0=$APPDIR/usr/lib/gstreamer-1.0` unconditionally, but with the default `bundleMediaFramework: false` that directory is never created.** Pointing that env var at a missing/empty dir disables GStreamer's default plugin search entirely, so WebKit's media setup fails ("GStreamer element appsink not found"), a NULL signal connect follows (GLib-GObject-CRITICAL), and the render process dies. It also rebuilds `~/.cache/gstreamer-1.0/registry.x86_64.bin` as an empty registry, which then affects other GStreamer apps for the user until the cache is cleared.

**3. (latent) The bundled WebKit helper binaries only have `RUNPATH=$ORIGIN`.** Any launch path that doesn't go through AppRun's `cd $APPDIR/usr` makes the bundled `libwebkit2gtk` (2.44 in my build) spawn the *system* WebKit helpers (2.52 on my machine) — the version mismatch kills WebKitNetworkProcess with SIGBUS.

### Reproduction

Build any tauri 2.11 app with `tauri build --bundles appimage` on ubuntu-24.04 with default config, then run the AppImage on Ubuntu 26.04 under Wayland. The window never appears and stderr shows the EGL abort above.

Manually removing the over-bundled infra libs from the extracted AppDir (`libwayland-*`, `libglib-2.0`/`libgio-2.0`/`libgobject-2.0`/`libgmodule-2.0`, all `libgst*`, `libmount`/`libblkid`/`libselinux`/`libpcre2-8`, `libzstd`/`libelf`/`libffi`) and symlinking `usr/lib/gstreamer-1.0` to the system plugin directory produces a fully working app on the same machine, so the system versions are drop-in compatible — bundling them is what breaks things.

### What I'd like

- A supported way to exclude libraries from the AppImage bundle (an `excludeLibraries`-style option under `bundle.linux.appimage`). Right now there's no config surface for this at all — `files` can only add real files, not remove or symlink — so we're stuck post-processing the AppImage in CI.
- Consider not bundling the wayland/glib infra by default (or adopting the community excludelist). Bundling them means every AppImage silently breaks on distros newer than the build machine, which defeats the point of building on the oldest supported base.
- AppRun probably shouldn't export `GST_PLUGIN_SYSTEM_PATH`/`GST_PLUGIN_SYSTEM_PATH_1_0` when `bundleMediaFramework` is false and the directory doesn't exist.

### Environment

- tauri / tauri-bundler 2.11.2 (I checked the diff through 2.11.5 — nothing touches this)
- Build: ubuntu-24.04 GitHub runner, `--bundles appimage`, no `bundle.linux` config
- Target: Ubuntu 26.04, GNOME Wayland, Mesa 25, GLib 2.88, webkit2gtk-4.1 2.52.3
- Real-world case: the block/buzz 0.3.45 release AppImage

title: RUSTSEC-2026-0204: Invalid pointer dereference in `fmt::Pointer` impl for `Atomic` and `Shared` when the underlying pointer is invalid
author: github-actions[bot]
body:
> Invalid pointer dereference in `fmt::Pointer` impl for `Atomic` and `Shared` when the underlying pointer is invalid

| Details             |                                                |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Package             | `crossbeam-epoch`                      |
| Version             | `0.9.18`                   |
| URL                 | [https://github.com/crossbeam-rs/crossbeam/pull/1276](https://github.com/crossbeam-rs/crossbeam/pull/1276) |
| Date                | 2026-07-06                         |
| Patched versions    | `>=0.9.20`                  |
| Unaffected versions | `<0.9.0`               |

Affected versions of `fmt::Display` dereference the underlying pointer. This causes a invalid pointer dereference e.g., when a pointer created with `Atomic::null` or `Shared::null`. `fmt::Debug` impls and pre-0.9 `fmt::Display` impls, which do not dereference pointers, are not affected by this issue.

See [advisory page](https://rustsec.org/advisories/RUSTSEC-2026-0204.html) for additional details.

title: [bug] iOS: embedded app extensions lose their entitlements when building with App Store Connect API-key auth
author: maccman
body:
### Describe the bug

When building an iOS app with `tauri ios build` using App Store Connect API-key authentication (`APPLE_API_KEY`/`APPLE_API_ISSUER`/`APPLE_API_KEY_CONTENT`), any **embedded app extension** (`PlugIns/*.appex`, e.g. a share extension) is exported **without its entitlements** — in our case the App Group entitlement was silently dropped, so the extension's `containerURL(forSecurityApplicationGroupIdentifier:)` returned nil at runtime and every share failed. The same project built without the API-key env vars (local Xcode account signing) produces a correct IPA.

The cause is visible in `crates/tauri-cli/src/mobile/ios/build.rs`: when credentials are present, the build/archive runs with `CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO`, and before export the CLI force-signs **only the main app binary** with a generated self-signed certificate to preserve its entitlements through `xcodebuild -exportArchive`:

```rust
// if we skipped code signing, we do not have the entitlements applied to our exported IPA
// we must force sign the app binary with a dummy certificate just to preserve the entitlements
// target.export() will sign it with an actual certificate for us
if skip_signing {
  ...
  self_signed_cert_keychain.sign(
    &app_dir.join(config.app().stylized_name()),   // <-- only the app binary
    Some(&...join(format!("{}.entitlements", config.scheme()))),
    false,
  )
```

`xcodebuild -exportArchive` preserves only entitlements that are already present in a signature, so the app comes out fine while every embedded `.appex` (left unsigned in the archive) is re-signed with just the profile defaults (`application-identifier`, `beta-reports-active`, `get-task-allow`, team id) — its declared entitlements (App Groups, etc.) are gone. The build, upload, and TestFlight processing all succeed, so nothing surfaces until the extension misbehaves at runtime.

Verified A/B on an unsigned device archive produced by `tauri ios build --archive-only --no-sign --ci`:

- appex left unsigned → exported appex entitlements: no `com.apple.security.application-groups`
- appex ad-hoc signed with its entitlements file before export (`codesign --force --sign - --entitlements ShareExtension/ShareExtension.entitlements ShareExtension.appex`) → exported appex keeps the App Group alongside the profile-derived keys

Suggested fix: in the `skip_signing` branch, after signing the app binary, also sign each `PlugIns/*.appex` (and presumably other nested code like watch apps) with its own target's entitlements file. Ad-hoc signing is sufficient for the entitlements to survive export — export re-signs everything with the real certificate anyway.

Our downstream workaround (works): a post-build phase on the extension target that ad-hoc signs the appex with its entitlements when `CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO` — team-reflect/reflect-open#612.

### Reproduction

1. iOS Tauri project with an embedded share-extension target (XcodeGen `app-extension` target with an `.entitlements` file declaring an App Group, embedded into the app target).
2. Build with API-key auth: `APPLE_API_KEY=... APPLE_API_ISSUER=... APPLE_API_KEY_CONTENT=... tauri ios build --export-method app-store-connect --ci`
3. Inspect the exported IPA: `unzip Reflect.ipa && codesign -d --entitlements :- Payload/*.app/PlugIns/*.appex` → the App Group entitlement is missing.
4. Build the same project without the API-key env vars (local Xcode account): the appex entitlements are intact.

### Expected behavior

Embedded app extensions keep the entitlements declared in their `.entitlements` files when building with App Store Connect API-key authentication, matching the behavior of locally-signed builds.

### Full `tauri info` output

```text
tauri-cli 2.11.3 (@tauri-apps/cli ^2.11.3)
macOS 26.5.1, Xcode 26.6 locally / Xcode 26.5 on GitHub macos-26 runners
```

### Stack trace

_No stack trace — the failure is silent at build time and only appears at runtime inside the extension._

### Additional context

Debugged end to end in team-reflect/reflect-open#612 (simulator repro with an entitlement-stripped appex, local export A/B, and CI verification with a pre-upload entitlements assertion).

title: [docs] Rust tracing spans documentation
author: linde12
body:
I'm using the `tracing` crate and have hooked it up to opentelemetry & Grafana, however handlers are being spawned in new tasks and don't know what span is the parent.

I have a usecase where i want to keep all logs & spans from the rust side of my application under a single parent representing an application session. E.g.:

```
GUI Run
  ComplexBusinessLogic
  SomeHTTPRequest
  MoreLogic
    SubLogic
```

but instead i get a root span that contains all sync command handlers as child spans, but each async command handler becomes its own root span.

How should we deal with this? Basically i want to do `async move { tauritasks.spawn(task); }.instrument(rootSpan)` but i have no clue how or if it's even possible

title: [bug] AppImage build stops at Running output plugin: appimage with no errors
author: pokeghosst
body:
### Describe the bug

When running `cargo tauri build`, the build process hangs at liine
```
-- Running output plugin: appimage --
```

With no further output. I've waited for 10 minutes or so just for a good measure but it seems that nothing is happening.

Interestingly, when running the build without internet connection, it doesn't stop at this line and continues, but fails (as expected) because appimagetool can't download the runtime since there's no connection.

title: [bug] [Linux/Wayland] window.add_child() child webview renders with incorrect bounds on Ubuntu 26.04 + WebKitGTK 2.52.3
author: hozaifa-ali
body: ### Describe the bug Child webview created with `window.add_child()` renders incorrectly on Linux Wayland. The webview overflows outside its intended area and covers the sidebar and side panel of the UI instead of staying in the content area between them. This works perfectly on macOS but is completely broken on Ubuntu 26.04 (Wayland) with WebKitGTK 2.52.3. No matter what coordinate system we use — Logical, Physical, manual `scale_factor()` multiplication — the position and size is always wrong. ### Reproduction 1. Create a Tauri 2 app with a React frontend webview filling the whole window 2. Add a sidebar (56px) and tab bar (40px) in the React UI 3. Call window.add_child() to spawn a child webview in the remaining content area 4. Pass bounds from getBoundingClientRect() on the content div 5. Run on Linux Ubuntu — child webview overflows over the sidebar and panels 6. Run same code on macOS — works perfectly Code used: window.add_child( builder, tauri::Position::Logical(tauri::LogicalPosition::new(x, y)), tauri::Size::Logical(tauri::LogicalSize::new(width, height)), ) Bounds come from getBoundingClientRect() with no DPR multiplication. ### Expected behavior The child webview should appear only inside the content area — to the right of the sidebar and below the tab bar — matching exactly the bounds passed to add_child(). Same behavior as macOS. ### Full `tauri info` output ```text [✘] Environment - OS: Ubuntu 26.4.0 x86_64 (X64) (ubuntu on wayland) ✔ webkit2gtk-4.1: 2.52.3 ✘ rsvg2: not installed ✔ rustc: 1.96.0 (ac68faa20 2026-05-25) ✔ cargo: 1.96.0 (30a34c682 2026-05-25) ✔ rustup: 1.29.0 (28d1352db 2026-03-05) ✔ Rust toolchain: stable-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu (default) - node: 24.18.0 - npm: 11.16.0 [-] Packages - tauri 🦀: 2.11.5 - tauri-build 🦀: 2.6.3 - wry 🦀: 0.55.1 - tao 🦀: 0.35.3 - @tauri-apps/api ⱼₛ: 2.11.1 - @tauri-apps/cli ⱼₛ: 2.11.4 [-] Plugins - tauri-plugin-fs 🦀: 2.5.1 - tauri-plugin-dialog 🦀: 2.7.1 - tauri-plugin-shell 🦀: 2.3.5 [-] App - build-type: bundle - framework: React - bundler: Vite - Running on Wayland ``` ### Stack trace ```text No crash or stack trace. The webview renders in the wrong position silently with no errors. ``` ### Additional context OS: Ubuntu 26.04 WebKitGTK: 2.52.3 (libwebkit2gtk-4.1-0) Rust: 1.96.0 Node: v24.18.0 We tried every coordinate approach: - Physical pixels with manual scale_factor() multiply = double scaled, wrong - Hardcoded inner_size() minus sidebar pixels = wrong when panel opens - LogicalPosition directly from getBoundingClientRect() = still overflows - Ratio scaling between JS viewport and native window = still off Root cause appears to be that on Linux/GTK, add_child() creates a separate GtkWindow instead of truly embedding the webview, so it uses a different coordinate space than the WebKit CSS pixels from getBoundingClientRect(). **Important:** running on Wayland (not X11). The tauri info output shows "ubuntu on wayland". This may be relevant since Wayland handles window coordinates differently from X11 and could be contributing to the add_child() bounds issue. Screenshot attached showing the overflow.
